does: This survey paper systematically catalogs and analyzes alignment goals for large language models across existing literature, tracing how these goals have evolved over time. It examines alignment from two complementary angles: how alignment goals are formally defined and how alignment is evaluated in practice. The paper identifies and organizes three distinct hierarchical levels of alignment goals, moving from lower-level instruction-following capabilities through higher-level human preference satisfaction toward deeper alignment with intrinsic human values. By mapping this landscape, the work reveals an underlying transformation in what researchers view as the fundamental target for LLM alignment, progressing from narrow task-specific objectives toward more comprehensive value alignment.
connects: This work builds on the growing body of research addressing LLM safety and alignment that emerged alongside scaling of language models. It responds to earlier work on instruction tuning, reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF), and human preference modeling that established foundational techniques for steering model behavior. The survey synthesizes insights from parallel research threads on constitutional AI, value learning, interpretability for alignment, and emerging capability evaluation methods. It also engages with philosophical and social science perspectives on defining human preferences and values that increasingly inform technical alignment approaches, connecting technical ML safety work with broader discussions about specification gaming, value misalignment, and societal impact of large models.
enables: By providing a structured taxonomy of alignment goals and their evolution, this survey enables researchers to make more informed choices about which alignment targets to pursue and how to evaluate success. It clarifies conceptual gaps where technical approaches may not adequately capture intended alignment goals, potentially directing future work toward more ambitious value alignment beyond instruction-following. The framework supports the development of more nuanced evaluation benchmarks that can test alignment at multiple levels. Additionally, the analysis of goal transformation suggests research directions for integrating multiple alignment perspectives, and it provides a foundation for cross-disciplinary collaboration between ML researchers, philosophers, and social scientists in defining and achieving robust long-term alignment of increasingly capable AI systems.
